evidence_quotes.quote: Consumer's rights of ownership in the face of IP licensing

paragraphs.text: Consumer's rights of ownership in the face of IP licensing
BART MICHAEL VAN DER STEEN
STUDENT LAW AT THE UNIVERSITY OF APPLIED SCIENCES WINDESHEIM, ALMERE, NETHERLANDS

evidence_quotes.quote: licenses that can be terminated unilaterally
revoke access with minimal legal consequences

paragraphs.text: The digital marketplace has created a troubling imbalance where consumers believe they "own" purchased content, yet companies routinely revoke access with minimal legal consequences. Despite paying full price, purchasers of digital media receive only licenses that can be terminated unilaterally, leaving them with no meaningful recourse, despite the EU's attempts at reconciling these problems. This submission examines the critical issue of digital content revocation from multiple perspectives to provide a comprehensive understanding of the challenges.

evidence_quotes.quote: Ubisoft's complete removal of videogame 'The Crew' from consumer libraries.

paragraphs.text: First, I will present the problem, documenting through the consumer's perspective specific cases where consumers lost, or were under threat of losing, access to digital goods they believed they owned. From Sony's planned deletion of Discovery content to Ubisoft's complete removal of videogame 'The Crew' from consumer libraries. These examples demonstrate how current practices violate consumer expectations and basic principles of fairness, leaving purchasers with no practical recourse despite clear monetary and non-monetary harm.

Second, I will address the corporate perspective, acknowledging the legitimate business challenges that companies face, including licensing complexities with third-party content providers, server maintenance costs for legacy products, and evolving technological standards that make long-term support increasingly difficult.

Finally, I will propose a carefully put together a possible solution to this systemic problem that could, potentially, address issues present for both consumer and license holder/distributor of said licenses. After that, I included a plea for the change of perspective on games as an artform to further illustrate why caution should be the norm when creating laws surrounding digital goods.

evidence_quotes.quote: led to believe they have purchased a product in the same way they purchase products from a physical store.
Users are often unaware of having purchased a license for the product, not the product itself.
Distributors have in practice often revoked or removed access to products these users purchased.

paragraphs.text: Users of online market spaces face continuous threats of revocation of their ‘purchased’ digital assets, often being led to believe they have purchased a product in the same way they purchase products from a physical store. Users are often unaware of having purchased a license for the product, not the product itself. Distributors have in practice often revoked or removed access to products these users purchased. Here are two examples for illustration:

evidence_quotes.quote: remove this content even from users who had purchased it, not merely subscribed to it
Access was contingent on corporate negotiations, not consumer rights

paragraphs.text: In 2023, Sony announced a plan to remove over 1,200 Discovery titles including *MythBusters*, *Deadliest Catch*, and *Cake Boss* from PlayStation users' libraries on December 31, citing "content licensing arrangements with content providers" as the reason. Critically, Sony would remove this content even from users who had purchased it, not merely subscribed to it. The company offered no compensation initially, and only after significant public backlash did Sony negotiate an extension with Warner Bros. Discovery was to maintain access for "at least the next 30 months". This temporary reprieve demonstrates the precariousness of digital "ownership": Access was contingent on corporate negotiations, not consumer rights (*Medina*, 2023).

section_path: Sony PlayStation's planned Discovery Content Deletion (2023)

paragraphs.text: While mostly being experienced as an issue in the US, the incident sparked widespread concern because it revealed the stark reality: consumers who clicked "Buy" had purchased nothing permanent. Sony's actions violated reasonable consumer expectations.

section_path: Sony PlayStation's planned Discovery Content Deletion (2023)

evidence_quotes.quote: Ubisoft's shutdown of *The Crew*, an online racing game.

paragraphs.text: Perhaps the most egregious example is Ubisoft's shutdown of *The Crew*, an online racing game. In March 2024, Ubisoft not only closed the game's servers but removed the game entirely from users' libraries, even from those who purchased physical copies. Users who attempted to launch the game found only a restricted demo or a demand for a new activation key.

evidence_quotes.quote: physical copies of *The Crew* included activation codes with expiration dates listed as 2099, implying the game would remain playable for decades.
consumers never possessed "unfettered ownership rights" but merely "a limited license to access the game".
This case crystallizes the core problem: companies use the word "buy" to suggest permanence, then invoke license agreements to justify revocation (*Carpenter*, 2025).

paragraphs.text: Two California plaintiffs filed a class-action lawsuit alleging violations of California's False Advertising Law, Unfair Competition Law, and Consumer Legal Remedies Act. In response, Ubisoft argued that consumers never possessed "unfettered ownership rights" but merely "a limited license to access the game". The company's EULA even demands that consumers "immediately uninstall the Product and destroy all copies" upon termination; an unprecedented requirement wherein consumers eliminate their own purchases. The lawsuit revealed that physical copies of *The Crew* included activation codes with expiration dates listed as 2099, implying the game would remain playable for decades. Yet Ubisoft shut it down after just ten years. This case crystallizes the core problem: companies use the word "buy" to suggest permanence, then invoke license agreements to justify revocation (*Carpenter*, 2025).

evidence_quotes.quote: Yet companies that delete or withhold purchased digital goods contravene the EU's stated cultural objective by impeding citizens' access to legally acquired works.

paragraphs.text: EU copyright law, especially the 2019 Directive on Copyright in the Digital Single Market, purports to "safeguard access to culture in the digital era" by harmonizing rights and facilitating cross-border access. Yet companies that delete or withhold purchased digital goods contravene the EU's stated cultural objective by impeding citizens' access to legally acquired works. The mismatch between EU copyright harmonization and consumer access failures may therefore justify regulatory corrections under cultural access provisions, rather than direct copyright litigation.

section_path: Friction with the current EU framework

evidence_quotes.quote: digital consumer remedies remain inadequate to address access revocation and withdrawal scenarios
not for revoked or withdrawn licenses.

paragraphs.text: Even recent EU frameworks such as the Digital Content Directive (EU 2019/770) and Digital Services Act provide remedies only for defective or nonconforming content, not for revoked or withdrawn licenses. The EU "Digital Fairness Fitness Check" (2024) acknowledged this gap, finding that "digital consumer remedies remain inadequate to address access revocation and withdrawal scenarios".

section_path: Friction with the current EU framework

evidence_quotes.quote: When a distribution agreement ends, those licenses often become void, leaving users without any legal means to reclaim what they've paid for.
In effect, consumers do not own their digital goods; they merely rent them at the mercy of corporate agreements that can, dramatically speaking, vanish overnight.

paragraphs.text: What we see is a predictable evolution in how digital goods interact with intellectual property law: licensors license distribution rights to intermediaries, who then sub-license access to consumers. Yet this framework systematically undermines consumer control. Once a purchase is made, consumers still depend entirely on the decisions of licensors and distributors for continued access to what they've paid for. When a distribution agreement ends, those licenses often become void, leaving users without any legal means to reclaim what was once available to them. In effect, consumers do not own their digital goods; they merely rent them at the mercy of corporate agreements that can, dramatically speaking, vanish overnight. This is deeply undesirable, especially when the affected goods are easily classifiable as works of art. Cultural creations should remain accessible to the public, not vanish because of a contract dispute.

section_path: Friction with the current EU framework

evidence_quotes.quote: gaming industry

paragraphs.text: Although partisan, I attempt in this chapter to accurately depict corporate and distributor issues with the problem at hand, to make the proposed approach more accessible. I will restrict myself to the gaming industry, due to the power of illustration it provides.

evidence_quotes.quote: gaming industry

paragraphs.text: The corporate and distributor side faces equally genuine constraints that are often invisible to consumers, especially in the incredibly competitive gaming industry. These limitations arise not from malice but from the complex web of intellectual property law, licensing economics, and technical realities that govern digital distribution.

evidence_quotes.quote: Racing games like Forza Horizon for example, contain licensed vehicles from manufacturers like Ferrari, Pagani, and Chevrolet, as well as licensed music tracks and real-world locations.

paragraphs.text: Game publishers and distributors frequently do not own all the intellectual property within their games. Racing games like Forza Horizon for example, contain licensed vehicles from manufacturers like Ferrari, Pagani, and Chevrolet, as well as licensed music tracks and real-world locations. Each of these requires separate licensing agreements with finite terms, typically ranging from 5-10 years (Bainbridge, 2025).

When these licenses expire, publishers face a difficult choice: renegotiating at potentially higher costs or delisting the game entirely. Renegotiation is often economically unviable for older titles that no longer generate significant revenue. Publishers do not choose these constraints; they inherit them from IP holders who protect their brands across multiple industries.

evidence_quotes.quote: Additionally, many games have servers active with which players can play the game jointly.

paragraphs.text: Additionally, many games have servers active with which players can play the game jointly. These servers cost money to maintain, so when players have reduced to an amount that makes it unviable to continue running said servers, the decision has to be made to shut them off. Consumers have pleaded for companies to hand over the reins in terms of server management, but most companies have resisted this, largely due to intellectual property control, cheating risks, and monetization models that depend on centralized infrastructure.

evidence_quotes.quote: live service games or games with gambling elements within them

paragraphs.text: The financial results after game development creates additional pressures. Most game revenue occurs early in the product lifecycle, with sales declining sharply after the first year. Maintaining server infrastructure for older games with dwindling player bases becomes economically unsustainable, with costs ranging from hundreds to thousands of euros monthly depending on player counts and infrastructure needs. This in turn could unveil the reason the larger publishers and creators of games resort to live service games or games with gambling elements within them: they generate revenue over their entire lifetime instead of the initial revenue generated upon purchase (Sancho-bosch 2024).
Having illustrated constraints on both sides of the issue, I hereby present a possible solution to address these issues.

Under the Rental and Lending Rights Directive (2006/115/EC), authors hold exclusive rights to authorize or prohibit the public lending of their works. But Article 6 of the directive allows EU Member States to create exceptions for public lending, as long as authors are fairly remunerated. This creates a statutory exception that enables public libraries, educational institutions, and archives to lend physical books and, in some cases, e-books without violating copyright.

evidence_quotes.quote: publishers of software and games

paragraphs.text: This could provide a solution for publishers of software and games: there already is an archive that sees to the preservation of media in all forms called the 'Internet Archive' as well as the lesser known, European owned Eurpeana. These platforms could be leveraged or legitimized to give distributors and licensors an out when server costs and licensing agreements become too burdensome, while retaining access to consumers when those are faced with deletion or revocation of their purchases.

One such archive might have to be allowed to lend multiple copies of these digital goods at once, as a proportional adaptation justified by the public-interest mission of libraries and the evolving nature of digital preservation and access recognized in EU legislation.

This approach would maintain fidelity to the EU's copyright balance under the Three-Step Test (Article 5(5) of the InfoSoc Directive), as the lending of multiple copies by a nonprofit cultural institution:
Serves a special purpose (education, cultural preservation, democratic access).
Does not conflict with the normal exploitation of the work where no legitimate market substitute exists or the publisher has withdrawn the work.

evidence_quotes.quote: public lending compensation under article 6(1) of Directive 2006/115/EC

paragraphs.text: Provides no unreasonable prejudice to the legitimate interests of the rightsholder (when fair remuneration structures (such as public lending compensation under article 6(1) of Directive 2006/115/EC) are preserved, as would be the case with libraries)

Digital preservation law already acknowledges that multiple reproductions are sometimes necessary to fulfil a public-interest mandate, as reflected in Article 6 of the Digital Single Market Directive (2019/790). The "one copy, one user" rule seems a pragmatic limit, not a moral boundary, designed to mirror physical scarcity and not to recreate it artificially in a digital space. As digital preservation doctrine already recognizes the necessity of multiple safeguarded copies (for integrity and accessibility), the same principles can justify controlled multi-copy digital lending as a lawful extension of the public lending right. This would modernize access obligations without diminishing author remuneration or eroding copyright protections.

evidence_quotes.quote: by making online features or internet access an inherent necessity for access.

paragraphs.text: Therefore, a well-arranged set of rules could provide the solution needed, without heavily infringing on the intellectual property rights of the licensors. If a rule were to be instated where if the intellectual property is used in a product like a game and it's been X years since date of publication, licensors have to provide a copy of their product to the relevant archive (like Europeana). Important would therefore be to consider that companies may tactically complicate this process by making online features or internet access an inherent necessity for access. This ought to be shielded from as well.

evidence_quotes.quote: describe games as a “genuine total art form”

paragraphs.text: Recent European Parliament resolutions, such as the 2022 draft report from the Committee on Culture and Education, finally describe games as a “genuine total art form” combining cinema, music, and interactive storytelling and call for the preservation of important European games as cultural heritage. Despite this progress, such language has yet to be codified into consistent EU cultural funding or copyright frameworks, leaving many lawmakers still regarding the medium as an economic sector, not an artistic one.

Culturally, gaming has long been stigmatized as a juvenile or antisocial pastime. Studies show many adults still view video games as “children’s stuff,” and gamers often avoid disclosing their hobby publicly for fear of being perceived as immature or lazy (Smarr-Foster, 2017). Until recently, universities and museums tended to exclude games from serious art or culture studies bias still present today despite growing recognition from cultural bodies and the status as a globally significant industry (Moss, 2018), projected to generate approximately 188.8 billion USD, which converts to €177 billion euros at the time of writing (Crichton-Stuart 2025), showing that more and more people are willing to actively participate in this artform.

evidence_quotes.quote: Games in particular hold a distinctly different position in respect to most other digital goods: they are forms of art that can be directly interacted with, often made by a lot of people who work in unison to create an immersive and provocative work.

There are many games that, after publication, are also designed to allow people with the required skillset to modify the game in any way they please so long as it is not done with a commercial purpose.

paragraphs.text: Games in particular hold a distinctly different position in respect to most other digital goods: they are forms of art that can be directly interacted with, often made by a lot of people who work in unison to create an immersive and provocative work. The enjoyment of games is reliant on the user’s direct involvement and input with the artform constituting the sum of its parts, or as the artist meant for the audience to enjoy. The interaction, however, doesn’t stop with playing games. There are many games that, after publication, are also designed to allow people with the required skillset to modify the game in any way they please so long as it is not done with a commercial purpose.

evidence_quotes.quote: Take for example one of the oldest, most well-known retro games, Doom, from 1993.
This game is still ‘modded’ to this day.
a playable level called ‘MyHouse’

paragraphs.text: Take for example one of the oldest, most well-known retro games, Doom, from 1993. This game is still ‘modded’ to this day. In a recent modification, or ‘WAD’, a user made a playable level called ‘MyHouse’ (sic.), a map that was directly inspired by Mark Z. Danielewski’s 2000
novel House of Leaves. In that book, a family's house is discovered to be impossibly larger on the inside than the outside, symbolizing the instability of perception, grief, and memory.

evidence_quotes.quote: video games

paragraphs.text: Many players and critics identify MyHouse as a powerful example of how video games, even running on 30-year-old technology, can embody the same literary and artistic themes as experimental fiction, by transforming an old shooting game into a surreal meditation on death, spatial distortion, and human emotion.

evidence_quotes.quote: The perception of games as "silly toys" or "dangerous distractions" remains a barrier to serious cultural treatment.

paragraphs.text: The perception of games as "silly toys" or "dangerous distractions" remains a barrier to serious cultural treatment, even as the medium produces works like the mod mentioned but also huge games like 'Baldur's Gate 3' by Belgian studio Larian Studios and 'Cyberpunk 2077' by Polish studio CD Projekt Red (recently known to be at the forefront of the gaming space) which rival literary and cinematic artistry in emotional and conceptual depth.

I plead therefore that the lawmaker takes this proposed solution into consideration, not just as consumer versus corporation, but artist versus restrictions, as the tale still often goes. Europeana and other similar archives provide a great framework for art to be enjoyed throughout the Union. With large studios in Europe's name such as the previously mentioned CD Projekt Red and Larian Studio's but also Ubisoft (France) and Paradox Interactive, it would truly be a shame to not award their creative works the space and access they rightfully deserve.
Thank you kindly, to those who read this document.

Bainbridge, J. (2025) A guide to intellectual property rights in the gaming industry (2025), Harper James. Available at: https://harperjames.co.uk/article/gaming-ip-rights/ (Accessed: 17 October 2025).
